topic: Indian Activist Forcibly Removed by Police While on Hunger Strike
headline: POLICE FORCIBLY REMOVE INDIAN ACTIVIST ON HUNGER STRIKE PROTESTING NEW ANTI-CORRUPTION LAW
summary: An Indian activist is currently on a hunger strike, protesting against a new anti-corruption law. The activist has been forcibly removed by police. Protests against the citizenship law continue across India, with demonstrators from various ages, castes, and religions participating. Police have been using barricades to block the protesters' marches.

summary_sources.title: Hunger strikes: What can they achieve? - BBC News
Framed and hanged: How the Indian state persecutes dissent
India: largest protests in decades signal Modi may have gone too far | India | The Guardian

sections.paragraph: The history of activism, particularly through hunger strikes, dates back to ancient times, with one of the first recorded instances found in ancient Indian scriptures. In pre-Christian Ireland, hunger strikes were even part of the legal system, where a person wronged by another could starve themselves to death on the wrongdoer's doorstep, placing a burden on them. The tactic has been used throughout history, including by Indian activist Mahatma Gandhi, who effectively used non-violent protest to undermine British rule in India. More recently, Indian social activist Anna Hazare has used hunger strikes to pressure the government, leading to the removal of corrupt officials and the passage of the Right to Information Act. Hazare's activism began after his service in the army and has focused on promoting rural development and governmental transparency, with his work in Ralegan Siddhi serving as a model for similar efforts in other villages. The use of hunger strikes as a form of protest has been employed by various groups, including suffragettes in Britain, who gained media attention and enhanced supporter commitment through their actions.

sources.title: Hunger strikes: What can they achieve? - BBC News
Anna Hazare | Indian Social Activist & Anti-Corruption Advocate | Britannica

sections.paragraph: The Indian government's handling of protests against the new citizenship law has sparked widespread criticism and raised concerns about the country's democratic values. Protesters, who come from diverse backgrounds and age groups, have been met with police barricades and violence, with some being portrayed as 'enemies' by government leaders. The situation escalated into violence on February 24, resulting in 53 deaths and hundreds of injuries, with the police being accused of reluctance to act against those inciting violence. The government's response to the protests has been seen as an attempt to suppress dissent, with many protesters, including young Muslim women and men, being arrested and detained. The protests have been characterized as a 'battle for democracy, a battle for civil liberties, a battle for secularism and the plural character of Indian society', with many believing that the government may have gone too far in its attempts to push through the citizenship law.

sources.title: India: largest protests in decades signal Modi may have gone too far | India | The Guardian
Framed and hanged: How the Indian state persecutes dissent

sections.paragraph: The social impact of hunger strikes is a significant aspect of the phenomenon, with prisoners using the refusal of food as a means to challenge various conditions of confinement, including arbitrary treatment, solitary confinement, and poor medical care. Throughout history, hunger strikers have faced severe consequences, including force-feeding procedures that have resulted in deaths, such as those of Abdul Qader Abu al-Fahm, Rasem Halawah, and Ali Jafari. The use of hunger strikes as a form of resistance is not limited to one region or group, with examples including Palestinian prisoners, Nelson Mandela, and Indian political prisoners. These individuals, often faceless to the general public, have become torchbearers for liberty and resistance against tyranny. Meanwhile, in a different context, India's jobs crisis has led to a situation where unemployment is a luxury that only the educated and relatively well-off can afford, while the poor and unskilled are compelled to take up any available work, highlighting the complexities of social and economic issues. The struggles of hunger strikers and the Indian workforce share a common thread of resistance and the pursuit of dignity and justice.

sources.title: The hunger strike: Where food is more than nourishment
India's jobs crisis is more serious than it seems

sections.paragraph: Recent legislation and proposed laws in various countries have raised concerns about the impact on human rights, particularly the right to peaceful protest. In the UK, advocacy groups like Liberty have expressed concern over measures to curb protests and activism, which they see as an attack on freedom of expression and assembly, protected under the European Convention on Human Rights. Similarly, in India, the Citizenship Amendment Act has been accused of discriminating against Muslims, leading to nationwide protests and a crackdown by authorities, with the UN raising concerns about excessive force used against students. The Indian government's response to the protests has been criticized as a declaration of war on Muslim citizens, forwarding a Hindu nationalist agenda. Meanwhile, in the UK, a European rights body has urged ministers to review protest laws, including a measure that restricts gatherings near places of worship, which could make it difficult to organize protests in large cities. These developments highlight the tension between government efforts to maintain order and the need to protect fundamental human rights.
